title: Moroccan carrot salad
subtitle: Cooked carrot salad with cumin and lemon
excerpt: Carrots cooked until just tender, then dressed warm with garlic, cumin, paprika, lemon juice, and olive oil. Served at room temperature as one of the Moroccan first-course salad plates alongside zaalouk and taktouka. The cumin is the defining flavour.
sourceNotes: Cooked carrot salad is one of the standard salad plates of the Moroccan first course. The dressing of cumin, garlic, and lemon is the same flavour combination used across Moroccan vegetable salads. Regional variations add harissa, preserved lemon, or coriander seed.

content.text: The carrots should be cooked until just tender but not collapsing. They should hold their cut shape in the finished salad. Dress them while still warm so they absorb the lemon and olive oil. The salad is served at room temperature, not hot and not cold.

Cut the carrots into rounds rather than matchsticks, the rounds hold up better and give more substance to the salad. A generous amount of fresh coriander is not optional: it provides the contrast to the warm spicing that lifts the whole dish.

Cook the {{carrot}} rounds in boiling salted water for 12 to 15 minutes until just tender. Drain well. While still warm, toss with the {{olive-oil}}, {{lemon-juice}}, {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Stir to coat evenly. Leave to cool to room temperature for at least 30 minutes, tossing occasionally as the carrots absorb the dressing. Stir in the {{coriander}} just before serving. Taste and adjust seasoning, the salad may need more lemon or salt as it cools.

items.fix: Always taste again at room temperature and add more lemon juice and salt as needed. The dressing that tasted correct when warm will often need boosting once cold
items.cause: Salt and acid diminish as the salad cools

items.fix: Check from 10 minutes, carrots should have a little resistance when pierced. Cut at 5 mm minimum thickness
items.cause: Boiled too long or cut too thin

items.fix: Dress the carrots while still warm and hot from draining, the heat opens the surface and allows the dressing to penetrate

content.text: Moroccan carrot salad is a constant on the salad-course table alongside zaalouk and taktouka. It is served with bread before the main dish arrives. It is one of the simplest Moroccan plates: a boiled vegetable dressed with the standard cumin, garlic, and lemon. It needs almost no skill to make well. Outside Morocco it is a useful side dish for grilled meats and fish, and for building a spread for a casual Moroccan meal at home.
